description: While no exact match for the proposed paper-based electrochemical biosensor for CRP in whole blood without preprocessing was found, several reviews highlight the development and potential of similar electrochemical biosensors for biomarker detection, including CRP, in point-of-care settings. These papers discuss the advantages of such sensors (rapidity, sensitivity, cost-effectiveness) but also acknowledge challenges in clinical validation and real-world matrices.

references.title: Electrochemical Aptamer-Based Biosensors for Sepsis Diagnosis: Recent Advances, Challenges, and Future Perspectives (2020-2025).
references.snippet: Sepsis remains a global health emergency, demanding timely and accurate diagnostics to reduce morbidity and mortality. This review critically assesses the recent progress (2020-2025) in the development of electrochemical aptamer-based biosensors for sepsis detection. These biosensors combine aptamers' high specificity and modifiability with the sensitivity and miniaturization potential of electrochemical platforms. The analysis highlights notable advances in detecting key sepsis biomarkers, such as C-reactive protein (CRP), procalcitonin (PCT), interleukins (e.g., interleukin-6 (IL-6), tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α)), lipopolysaccharides (LPSs), and microRNAs using diverse sensor configurations, including a field-effect transistor (FET), impedance spectroscopy, voltammetry, and hybrid nanomaterial-based systems. A comparative evaluation reveals promising analytical performance in terms of the limit of detection (LOD), rapid response time, and point-of-care (POC) potential. However, critical limitations remain, including variability in validation protocols, limited testing in real clinical matrices, and challenges in achieving multiplexed detection. This review underscores translational barriers and recommends future directions focused on clinical validation, integration with portable diagnostics, and interdisciplinary collaboration. By consolidating current developments and gaps, this work provides a foundation for guiding next-generation biosensor innovations aimed at effective sepsis diagnosis and monitoring.
references.description: This review summarizes advances from 2020-2025 in electrochemical aptamer-based biosensors for sepsis detection, focusing on biomarkers like CRP, PCT, and interleukins. It evaluates their performance in terms of LOD, response time, and POC potential, while also noting limitations in validation protocols and testing with real clinical matrices.
references.importance: The paper directly addresses electrochemical biosensors for CRP and discusses their potential for point-of-care diagnostics, aligning with the hypothesis's core subject. It also highlights the challenge of limited testing in real clinical matrices, which the hypothesis explicitly aims to address by using whole blood without preprocessing.

references.title: Electrochemical Detection of Cancer Biomarkers: From Molecular Sensing to Clinical Translation.
references.snippet: Early cancer detection is crucial for improving survival rates and treatment outcomes. Electrochemical biosensors have emerged as powerful tools for early cancer detection due to their high sensitivity, specificity, and rapid detection capabilities. This review explores recent advancements (2015-2025) in electrochemical biosensors for cancer biomarker detection, their working principles, novel nanomaterial-based enhancements, challenges, and prospects for clinical applications. Specifically, we highlight the electrochemical detection of protein biomarkers (e.g., CEA, PSA, CRP), nucleic acid markers (ctDNA, miRNA, methylation patterns), and metabolic indicators, emphasizing their clinical relevance in early diagnosis and monitoring. Unlike previous reviews which focus on either biomarker classes or sensor platforms, this review uniquely integrates both factors. This review provides a novel perspective on how next-generation electrochemical biosensors can bridge the gap between laboratory development and real-world cancer diagnostics.
references.description: This review from 2026 explores advancements (2015-2025) in electrochemical biosensors for cancer biomarker detection, covering working principles, nanomaterial enhancements, and clinical applications. It specifically mentions the electrochemical detection of protein biomarkers, including CRP, and discusses the transition from laboratory development to real-world diagnostics.
references.importance: This review supports the feasibility of using electrochemical biosensors for detecting protein biomarkers like CRP, a central component of the hypothesis. Its focus on clinical translation and bridging the gap to real-world diagnostics provides context for the hypothesis's goal of a clinically relevant, rapid detection method.

references.title: Smart microfluidic devices integrated in electrochemical point-of-care platforms for biomarker detection in biological fluids.
references.snippet: The increasing demand for decentralized, accessible, and rapid analytical tools is driving a transformation in healthcare toward point-of-care (POC) analytical technologies. The final aim is to reduce the cost of healthcare management originating from frequent patient hospitalizations and expensive and time-consuming laboratory-based analyses. This review explores the integration of microfluidic technologies with electrochemical sensing platforms, aiming to address the urgent need for POC analytical platforms. Owing to the miniaturization of fluid management systems and exploiting fluid automation, microfluidic devices enable low sample consumption, cost-effective analysis, and multiplexed detection, offering promising tools for real-time health monitoring. Among the other materials, the most commonly used substrates for microfluidics fabrication are paper, PDMS, and adhesive tape, which support custom-designed microchannel architectures, passive fluid motion, and wearable integration. Special attention is given to wearable sensors for sweat analysis, with various approaches employing capillary-driven flows and smart microfluidic designs to enable continuous and autonomous monitoring of biomarkers. Highlighting relevant works from the last 5 years, the review explores the role of integrated microfluidic electrochemical sensing devices in delivering advanced decentralized analytical platforms, with significant potential for clinical use in biomarker detection.
references.description: This review covers the integration of microfluidic technologies with electrochemical sensing platforms for point-of-care applications, emphasizing the use of paper as a common substrate for microfluidics. It highlights how these devices enable low sample consumption, cost-effective, and multiplexed detection for biomarker monitoring.
references.importance: The review directly addresses the 'paper-based' aspect of the hypothesis's sensor format and its application in point-of-care electrochemical platforms. It supports the feasibility and advantages of using paper-based microfluidics for biomarker detection, providing a good general background for the technology proposed in the hypothesis.

tavily_query: Paper-based electrochemical biosensor anti-CRP antibodies detection limit whole blood
summary: The hypothesis proposes a novel application of a paper-based electrochemical biosensor for rapid, high-sensitivity, and preprocessing-free CRP detection in whole blood, aiming to match ELISA performance. While prior work exists on electrochemical biosensors for CRP and paper-based microfluidics, the specific combination of no preprocessing for whole blood, clinically relevant sensitivity (<0.5 mg/L), and rapid time-to-result (within 10 minutes) appears to be an under-explored, critical area. The identified literature consists primarily of reviews that acknowledge the potential of such systems but also highlight the challenges in real-world clinical validation for point-of-care devices. This suggests the hypothesis addresses a specific gap in translating these technologies to practical, direct-from-sample clinical use.
